facts.text: Astoria grew from a 17th‑century fort site called Hallet's Cove, later renamed after fur trader John Jacob Astor to attract investment.
The Steinway & Sons piano factory, founded in Manhattan in 1853, moved to Astoria in the 1870s, creating a model company town with housing, a church, and a streetcar line.
Astoria hosts the Museum of the Moving Image, a hub for film and TV history that showcases the neighborhood’s long‑standing role on screen.
The neighborhood’s waterfront features Socrates Sculpture Park, founded in 1986 on a reclaimed landfill, now a world‑class open‑air art space.
Astoria’s oldest beer garden, Bohemian Hall, opened in 1910 when the area was largely Irish, Italian, Czech, and Slovak.
The WPA‑era Astoria Pool, designed by Aymar Embury II and opened in 1936, became a landmark for Olympic trials, concerts, and summer afternoons.
